title: Chapter 4 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Loops and Iterators! Can you avoid the infinite loop?

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a loop?
options: To jump skipping lines of code.
To execute a block of code repeatedly without writing it out multiple times.
questions.explanation: Loops allow you to run the exact same piece of code hundreds of times effortlessly.

questions.question: When does a `while` loop stop running?
options: When it finishes iterating over a list.
questions.explanation: A `while` loop continues executing *while* its condition remains `true`. The moment it hits `false`, it stops.

questions.question: What happens if a `while` loop condition NEVER becomes false?
options: It acts as a normal loop.
It creates an infinite loop and runs forever until the program crashes or is manually killed.
questions.explanation: If the condition never evaluates to false, your loop will run infinitely.

questions.question: What is the opposite of a `while` loop?
questions.explanation: An `until` loop runs continuously *until* its condition becomes true.

questions.question: When does an `until` loop stop running?
options: When its internal counter hits zero.
questions.explanation: The loop is saying "Keep going UNTIL this thing happens (becomes true)."

questions.question: In Ruby, numbers are primitive values and do not have built-in methods.
questions.explanation: Everything is an object in Ruby! Numbers have wonderful built-in methods, such as `.times`.

questions.question: If you just want to run a block of code exactly 5 times, what is the cleanest Ruby syntax?
questions.explanation: The `.times` method attached to an integer is the perfect, cleanest way to simply repeat code a fixed number of times.

questions.question: Which keywords are used to define the block of code attached to an iterator like `.times`?
questions.explanation: Ruby uses `do` to start a block, and `end` to close it.

questions.question: What is the punctuation mark used to pass block parameters (like `|index|`) into the loop?
questions.explanation: The pipe character `|` catches individual items generated by the iterator.

questions.question: When you use `.times do |index|`, what number does the `index` counting begin on by default?
questions.explanation: Computer programming is strictly 0-indexed. Sequence counting almost always begins from zero.

questions.question: What does a sequence of two dots `..` represent in Ruby?
options: An inclusive Range from the start number to the end number.
An exclusive Range that skips the end number.
questions.explanation: Two dots `..` create an inclusive Range object (e.g., `1..5` includes the number 5).

questions.question: What does a sequence of THREE dots `...` represent?
options: An inclusive Range from the start number to the end number.
An exclusive Range that STOPS right before the end number.
questions.explanation: Three dots `...` create an exclusive Range (e.g., `1...5` only goes up to 4).

questions.question: To grab each element out of a sequence one-by-one, professional Rubyists vastly prefer the `_____` iterator method over a `for` loop.
questions.explanation: The `.each` method is the gold standard for iteration in Ruby.

questions.question: Why are iterators (like `.each`) considered better than traditional loops (like `for`) in Ruby?
options: Because you don't need to create random external tracking variables; iterators inject values straight into a block.
Iterators aren't better, `for` loops are preferred.
questions.explanation: Iterators abstract away the ugly clunky logic of traditional looping, letting you just focus on passing data into a clean block.

questions.question: If you want to use the single-line syntax for a block, you replace `do` and `end` with what?
questions.explanation: Curly braces define an inline (single-line) block. E.g., `(1..5).each {|n| puts n}`

questions.question: Which keyword lets you COMPLETELY ESCAPE and destroy an ongoing iteration loop?
questions.explanation: The `break` keyword immediately permanently terminates the loop it's inside of.

questions.question: Which keyword skips the current iteration and instantly leaps to the START of the exact next iteration?
questions.explanation: The `next` keyword abandons the current cycle, going straight to the next one.

questions.question: The `for` loop automatically creates an exclusive range under the hood.
questions.explanation: A `for` loop just processes the exact data you feed it. If you feed it an inclusive range `1..5`, it includes `5`.

questions.question: What is the output of `3.times { puts "cat" }`?
questions.explanation: It prints "cat" three times in a row.

questions.question: Ruby iterators like `.times` and `.each` both pass control directly to a _____ of code.
questions.explanation: A chunk of code wrapped in `do..end` or `{..}` is formally called a Block.
